text: BEHAVIOR 1HIzRAPY 20, 499-508, 1989
Anxiety Disorders in Children: A Multiple-Baseline
Evaluation of a Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment
MARTHA T. KANE
PHILIP C. KENDALL
Chdd and Adolescent Anxiety Dtsorders Climc
Temple Umversity

Four children diagnosed with Overanxious Disorder were treated individually with 
a 16-20 session, cognitive-behavioral treatment that was evaluated using assessments 
from multiple sources and a multiple-baseline across cases design for the self-reports. 
The children, aged 9-13 years, came from middle class backgrounds, and were within 
the limits of normal intelligence. Parent report, independent clinician's ratings, and 
child self-report were used to assess outcome. All children showed improvement on 
parent and independent clinician's ratings, as well as on self-report. Treatment-produced 
gains, as measured with parent and child report were, in general, mamtained at 3-6 
month follow-up. These outcomes suggest that a cognitive-behavioral approach to 
treating anxiety in children is useful, though a controlled comparison evaluation is 
needed.

There has been a growing concern accompanied by mounting evidence sug- 
gesting the need for interventions designed specifically for children. Indeed 
significant contributions have been made in terms of treatment strategies for 
the externalizing, aggressive, non-compliant, hyperactive, and impulsive child 
(e~g. Forehand & McMahon, 1981; Kendall & Braswell, 1985). However, treat- 
ment strategies and interventions for the internalizing, anxious, isolated, or 
withdrawn child have not been as fully developed. Kendall, Howard, and Epps 
(1988) noted the paucity of group comparison studies for childhood anxiety 
disorders.

Certainly children displaying significant levels of internalizing behaviors con- 
stitute a noticeable proportion of any child clinical population. Specifically, 
Morris and Kratochwill (1985) noted variable prevalence rates in various studies

the second author.

tinction has been called into question (Johnson & Melamed, 1979) and the state scale may be 
subject to the influence of specific events at the moment the child completes the measure.

500 
KANE AND KENDALL

of the incidence of clinical fears (<8070). Social withdrawal as evidenced by 
anxiety, isolation, hypersensitivity, depression and self-consciousness is seen 
in 10-20% of school-age children (Werry & Quay 1971). Recently, Kashani 
and Orvashel (1988) reported that 1707o of 14-16 year olds met the criteria for 
one or more anxiety disorders as defined in the DSM-III-R (APA, 1987) and 
8.707o displayed symptoms severe enough to warrant treatment.

Although a limited number of fears and worries represent normal growth 
and development, concern arises when childhood fears and worries come so 
severe as to interfere with appropriate activities or have a negative impact on 
the child's relationships within the family, peers, or broader social networks. 
The implications of such difficulties may be lifelong, particularly if such difficul- 
ties prevent the child from learning appropriate social skills. Recent work sug- 
gests the possibility that children who suffer from anxiety mature into adults 
who continue to suffer from anxious symptoms (Last, Phillips, & Statfield,

1987). Last et al. (1987) report that many adults suffering with agoraphobia 
and generalized anxiety disorder describe symptoms of separation anxiety and 
generalized anxiety during their childhood years.

Although the question of effective treatments for more generalized anxiety 
has received little systematic research attention, behavioral techniques have 
been found to be effective in treating children's specific fears and phobias (King, 
Hamilton, & Ollendick, 1988). In the search for treatments of generalized child- 
hood anxiety, investigators have drawn from the adult anxiety literature (see 
Barlow, 1988). As Strauss (1988) noted, generalized anxiety in adults has been 
successfully treated with various forms of behavior therapy. An effective treat- 
ment for generalized anxiety in children is hypothesized to be one that com- 
bines the demonstrated behavioral techniques with a focus on the cognitive 
components of anxiety. That there may be a cognitive component to child- 
hood anxiety has been suggested recently (Kendall, Howard & Epps, 1988; 
Francis, 1988). Those studies that have examined children's cognitions as part 
of treatment for anxiety disorders have suggested that cognitions are impor- 
tant in both assessment and treatment (e.g., Morris & Kratochw_ill, 1983). The 
purpose of the present study is to evaluate the efficacy of a cognitive-behavioral 
treatment for diagnosed anxiety disordered children.

METHOD 
Subjects

Four children, of at least average intelligence, with DSM-III-R diagnosed 
anxiety disorders completed the treatment protocol. Two children, a 10-year- 
old boy (JM) and a 13-year-old girl (DK), were diagnosed with Overanxious 
Disorder (Secondary Simple Phobia). The other two children were both 
9-year-old girls diagnosed with Overanxious Disorder (CL, AM).

Two children were referred by school counselors after experiencing difficul- 
ties with anxiety (DK and CL) and two were identified on group screenings 
(JM and AM). Subjects were assessed using self-report measures, followed 
by individual structured diagnostic interviews. All subjects scored more than 
one standard deviation above the mean on both self-report measures and met 
DSM-III-R criteria for diagnosis.

ANXIETY DISORDERS IN CHILDREN 
501
Measures

Anxiety Disorders Interview Schedule for Children (Silverman & Nelles, 
1988) is a structured interview schedule designed for the diagnosis of child- 
hood anxiety disorders using both parent and child report. The schedule allows 
the assessor to screen out other disorders and is consistent with current DSM- 
III-R criterion.

The State-Trait Anxiety Inventory for Children (STAIC) (Spielberger, 1973) 
includes two 20-item self report scales: enduring tendencies to experience 
anxiety (A-Trait), and temporal and situational variations in levels of anxiety 
(A-State). Normative, reliability, and validity data are available (Morris & 
Kratochwill, 1983).

Child Behavior Checklist (CBCL) (Achenbach & Edelbrock, 1983) is an 
ll8-item scale that assesses an array of behavioral problems and social com- 
petencies completed separately by parents and teachers.

State-Trait Anxiety Inventory for Children--Modification of Trait Version 
for Parents (A-Trait-P) (Strauss, 1987) is a modification of the trait version

of Spielberger's (1973) STAIC to be used for parent report. To date, there are 
no reliability or validity data available for this measure.

Hamilton Anxiety Rating Scale, a 14-item independent clinician rating, was 
used to assess symptoms of anxiety (Hamilton, 1960). Slight modifications were 
made in the wording of the descriptors to accommodate children's language 
development.

Progress on target complaints (initial significant behavioral problems) was 
monitored throughout.
Design

Treatment was initiated after baseline assessment periods of 1, 2, or 3 ses- 
sions to produce a multiple-baseline design (Kendall & Koehler, 1985). As- 
sessments were conducted at pretreatment, during treatment, posttreatment 
and again at follow-up.
Setting

The therapy was conducted through the Child and Adolescent Anxiety Dis- 
orders Clinic of Temple University. Some sessions were conducted at the child's 
school.
Intervention

Subjects received 16-20 one-hour, individual treatment sessions, meeting 
twice weekly except for illness and school holidays. The details of the treat- 
ment are specified in a treatment manual. The therapists were advanced 
graduate students who had participated in the development of the manual.

Children received training in the recognition and analysis of anxious cogni- 
tions and were taught to use them as cues for using anxiety management strate- 
gies to help them cope more effectively in anxiety-provoking situations. The 
treatment focuses on four major components: a) recognizing anxious feelings 
and somatic reactions to anxiety, b) clarifying cognitions in anxiety-provoking 
situations (i.e. unrealistic or negative attributions or expectations) c) developing

502 
KANE AND KENDALL

a plan to help cope with the situation (i.e. modifying anxious self-talk into 
coping self-talk as well as determining what coping actions might be effec- 
tive), and d) evaluating the success of the coping strategies and self-rein- 
forcement as appropriate.

Behavioral training strategies such as modeling, in vivo exposure, role play, 
relaxation training, and contingent reinforcement were incorporated. To help 
reinforce and generalize the skills, homework assignments were assigned. The 
training tasks and assignments were presented in a graduated sequence and 
were practiced in increasingly anxiety-provoking situations. Review of the pre- 
viously introduced skills was included in each session. Once the full comple- 
ment of skills has been acquired, these skills were practiced in imaginal and 
in vivo situations. Throughout the sessions, social reinforcement by the ther- 
apist was used to encourage and reward the child. In addition, the child was 
encouraged to verbally reinforce his own successful coping. The child was also 
urged to practice using the coping skills when anxiety provoking situations 
arose at home or at school.
Manipulation Check

An assessment of treatment integrity (Kazdin, 1986a) was completed via 
audiotapes. The second author listened to 20 randomly selected segments of 
randomly selected tapes (approximately 33°70 of the total number of tapes) 
and determined the integrity of the cognitive-behavioral procedures.

R ES U LTS 
The effects of the program were positive. For all children, parent CBCL evi- 
denced decreases from pre- to post-treatment on internalizing scales (See Table 
1). Most gains were substantial enough to bring the internalizing behaviors 
to within normal limits (i.e., t score < 70). Both JM and DK continued to have 
some somatic problems, but at reduced levels. Parent reports of child anxiety 
were also obtained via weekly A-Trait-P reports. Inspection of Table 2 sug- 
gests that from pre- to post-treatment there was a decrease in the parent's ratings 
of each child's trait anxiety. All parents reported overall decreases in anxiety.

Independent clinician's ratings on the Hamilton indicated noticeable de- 
creases in anxious symptoms (see Table 2). Based on the target complaints 
specific to each subject, the treatment produced decreases from at least 
moderate levels of anxiety to extremely mild levels. For example, CL initially 
had autonomic symptoms of anxiety such as sweating, tension headache, and 
dry mouth, which were reduced to very mild levels by the conclusion of treat- 
ment. Both JM and DK experienced gastrointestinal symptoms such as nausea 
in reaction to evaluation situations, which was substantially reduced by the 
treatment.

The children's A-Trait self-reports, using the multiple-baseline, evidenced 
change from pre- to post-treatment (see Figure 1). For each child, as the treat- 
ment was introduced across the multiple baseline, the reported levels of trait 
anxiety fell from the pretreatment level of at least one standard deviation above 
the mean, to a posttreatment level at or near the normative mean. As seen

in Figure 1, levels of anxiety remained fairly constant during the baseline as- 
sessment periods except for AM, whose scores dropped briefly but returned 
to criterion prior to the onset of treatment. After treatment was instituted, 
the subject's reported levels of anxiety decreased, although the pattern of de- 
crease differed among the children. JM reported a gradual decrease in trait 
anxiety levels over time, while both DK and AM reported fairly steady levels 
of anxiety until they had completed the first few in vivo exposure sessions. 
CL's reported levels of trait anxiety barely reached the criterion despite clear 
indications of anxiety on all other measures, and fell fairly quickly. Her reported 
levels of anxiety remained substantially below the mean for the remainder 
of treatment.

Target complaints for each child indicated behavior change. JM was ini- 
tially unable to sleep in the dark and would become ill and go home when 
stressed at school; he now sleeps with only a nightlight, and does not leave 
school. His mother noted that JM was trying to help a fearful cousin by teaching 
him the elements of the treatment. DK initially experienced high levels of evalu- 
ation anxiety; at the end of treatment she was able to win a scholarship by 
performing in a competition with an audience. AM's grandmother had to pick 
her up from school everyday because of her fear, but AM became able to take 
public transportation or walk home from school alone. CL suffered significant 
evaluation anxiety, but, according to both parent and teacher reports, was able 
to concentrate and prepare for tests following treatment.

Teacher CBCL reports (see Table 3) for DK and CL indicated that pretreat- 
ment levels of internalizing behaviors were decreased to within normal ranges 
at posttreatment. Teachers' pretreatment reports for JM and AM indicated 
very low levels of internalizing and externalizing behaviors, and showed no 
change at posttreatment. Overall, teacher reports indicated lower levels of 
anxiety than other reports at both pretreatment and posttreatment. While the 
number of subjects in this sample is too small to draw any conclusion, addi- 
tional research is needed to clarify these discrepancies across reporting sources.
Follow-up data indicated that for 50°70 of the children (AM, CL), according

to both child and parent report, the anxious behaviors remained within normal 
ranges. For the other two children (DK, JM), the evidence of maintenance 
was qualified; the children's self-reports remained within normal ranges whereas 
parent's reports indicated some return of difficulty with anxious behaviors. 
JM's parent reported some internalizing problems, although not as problematic 
as at pretreatment. For DK, difficulties occurred on only those scales that had 
shown the least improvement at posttreatment.

DISCUSSION 
Although preliminary, the results suggest that a cognitive-behavioral treat- 
ment can be efficacious in the treatment of anxiety disorders in children. 
Changes were produced on parent report, independent clinician's rating, and 
child self-report from pretreatment to posttreatment. Target behaviors were 
significantly improved. The use of a multiple baseline design helped to reduce 
the likelihood that the effects are simply due to sources of internal invalidity, 
such as the passage of time and contact with a therapist. However, the multiple- 
baseline design has limitations (see Kazdin, 1982) as does the use of only self- 
report assessments from multiple sources. A controlled group comparison out- 
come study using multimethod assessments is needed to investigate the efficacy 
of various strategies for the treatment of childhood anxiety disorders.

Follow-up data suggested that treatment gains were maintained for two of 
the children as reported on both child and parent report. The other two chil- 
dren reported continued gains on child self-report, but parent report evidenced 
a return of some previous difficulties. It is interesting that these two children 
had secondary diagnoses of Simple Phobia; the impact of multiple diagnoses 
on treatment outcome and maintenance of treatment gains is an important

ANXIETY DISORDERS IN CHILDREN 
507

question for future research. In addition, conversations with DK's parents re- 
vealed that she had experienced a number of unresolved medical problems 
and that her cousin had recently died of cancer. Such life stresses are anxiety 
provoking, particularly for a young person, and in part her increased difficul- 
ties may have been due to these situations. The potential benefits of booster 
sessions merits future attention.

A number of questions arose during treatment. The children ranged in age 
from 9 through 13, and the younger children seemed to have trouble iden- 
tifying anxious cognitions in both imaginal and in vivo contexts, though they 
did report anxious ideation. They were able to discuss what might happen 
to another child in such a situation, but seemed unable to discuss their thoughts 
in terms of what might happen to them. Though tentative, these experiences 
suggest a developmental/age level below which anxious cognitions may be 
difficult to target. However, it is also possible that the inaccessibility of the 
self-talk was the result of a coping strategy being used by those particular chil- 
dren (C. Spielberger, personal communication, 1987).

Another issue concerns parental influence on the maintenance of anxious 
symptomology, and the implications for treatment strategies. Kendall, et al, 
(1988) noted that most behavioral patterns and cognitive interpretations have 
little or no meaning except within the context of social/interpersonal func- 
tioning. In each case the children expressed anxieties related to parental ex- 
pectations and pressures or concerns about the welfare of their parent(s). Cer- 
tain children found it difficult to alter their behavior patterns at home. Research 
is needed to study the role of family systems and specific parenting styles in 
the development and maintenance of anxiety disorders in children.
